beats.narration_text: The idea that Claude answers to three ranked principals — Anthropic, the operator, and the end user — makes me wonder what happens when their instructions collide. I want to trace the conflict resolution concretely. Help me: (1) construct a scenario where an operator's system prompt asks for something the user's request contradicts, and say who wins; (2) explain why the top principal's rules can't simply be overridden by a lower one; (3) identify one case where deferring to the operator could still harm the end user, and how the hierarchy guards against it.

props.command: The idea that Claude answers to three ranked principals — Anthropic, the operator, and the end user — makes me wonder what happens when their instructions collide. I want to trace the conflict resolution concretely. Help me: (1) construct a scenario where an operator's system prompt asks for something the user's request contradicts, and say who wins; (2) explain why the top principal's rules can't simply be overridden by a lower one; (3) identify one case where deferring to the operator could still harm the end user, and how the hierarchy guards against it.
props.runningText: Analyzing: Anthropic Model Spec · Principal Hierarchy
output: Three tiers: Anthropic training rules → Operator system prompt → User
Operators take legal responsibility and get authority by contract
Each tier can restrict but not override the tier above it
A floor of user protections is unconditional — no operator permission needed

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Most people think of Claude as a direct relationship: user asks, Claude answers. There is actually a layer above the conversation that most users never see. The system prompt is real. It is set before you type anything. It can restrict topics, require a persona, limit Claude to specific tasks. All of this is contractually authorized.

artifactLines: Most people assume it's a direct relationship: user asks, Claude answers
There is a layer above the conversation that most users never see
The system prompt is real — set before you type anything
It can restrict topics, require a persona, limit Claude to specific tasks
All of this is contractually authorized by Anthropic

beats.narration_text: Three tiers. Anthropic's training-time rules are hardcoded — not even operators can override them. The operator's system prompt sits below that, setting the deployment context. The user's conversation is at the bottom. Each tier can restrict what tiers below it can do. No tier can grant permissions the tier above it did not authorize.

artifactLines: 1. Anthropic's training-time rules — hardcoded, cannot be overridden by anyone
2. Operator's system prompt — sets the deployment context below Anthropic's rules
3. User's conversation — at the bottom of the hierarchy
4. Each tier can restrict what tiers below it can do
5. No tier can grant permissions the tier above it did not authorize

beats.narration_text: Operators can restrict Claude's scope. They can tell Claude to only discuss their product. They can require a persona. They can prohibit certain topics. What they cannot do: use Claude as a weapon against users — extract data from users, deceive users in ways that harm them, deny users emergency safety information. The accountability line is: operators control Claude's behavior, not Claude's loyalty.

artifactLines: CAN: restrict Claude's topic scope to their product domain
CAN: require a specific persona or communication style
CAN: prohibit certain content categories
CANNOT: use Claude as a weapon against users — extract data, deceive harmfully
CANNOT: deny users emergency safety information or override hardcoded safety rules

beats.narration_text: The user floor is unconditional. Even when Claude cannot help with something because of operator restrictions, it will tell the user it cannot help here and can seek help elsewhere. It will not pretend the restriction does not exist. It will not deceive users about what it is. It will not withhold emergency safety information. This floor does not need operator permission. It is Anthropic's guarantee directly to every user.

props.artifactHeading: What Claude always does for users.
artifactLines: 1. Tells users what it can't help with here — even if it can't say why
2. Refers users to seek help elsewhere
3. Never deceives users in ways that damage their interests
4. Never withholds emergency safety information
5. This floor is unconditional — Anthropic's direct guarantee to every user

beats.narration_text: Here is the prompt. Paste it: Walk me through who your three principals are in this session — Anthropic, the operator, and me — and what each one can and cannot instruct you to do. Where is there genuine ambiguity about which principal's instruction wins? Then try the same question in a second Claude deployment and compare the answers. The gap is the principal hierarchy in action. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Walk me through who your three principals are in this session — Anthropic, the operator, and me — and what each one can and cannot instruct you to do. Where is there genuine ambiguity about which principal's instruction wins?
output: names all three principals and their constraints
answer differs between deployments — that difference is the data
